title: 2026/03/23 - JU [Part 2]
description: Monday, 23 March 2026 | Justice Committee Arms Bill Hearing of evidence: David Zwartz Selwyn McCallum Simon Nelson Conrad Petersen John Knight David Allard Quentin Abraham Sam Somers Brigitte Grabowski Alison Joy Mace Allan Dittmer Daniel Tizzard-Close Evan Minton Leone Brown Dylan Woodhouse Lytton Volante Malcolm Lumsden Jeremy Rae Robert Young Craig Body Dr Chris Mansell Reuben Sankey Sienna Ditttmer Nick Newman
descriptionSummary: Arms Bill. David Zwartz. Selwyn McCallum

chunks.text: Jeg kan godt anbefale det.

Could we have Matthew's- No, he's not. He's supposed to be on the line. He's not there? He's- Right. David Schwartz? Yes. Hello. My name's- Um, is Rachel coming back here? I'm a Jew, so. Yeah. And Schwartz. I'm Rachel, who's for... Right. We've got five minutes with you, Mr. Schwartz.
Um- It'd be good to hear from you. We have seen your, submission, as you're probably aware. Yep.

Tena koutou katoa, Shalom. I'm a Jew, and I have special concerns for the safety of the New Zealand Jewish community, which is very similar to the concerns of the Muslim community, who I think you've heard from, and I support their submission to this bill from FIANZ, the Federation of Islamic Associations.

The New Zealand Jewish community has not suffered the same tragedy as the massacre of Muslim worshipers in Christchurch on 15th of March 2019, but the Jewish community is experiencing the same increase in anti-Jewish incidents as is happening worldwide.

New Zealand police figures show the highest ever number of such incidents over the last two years, and we have reason to fear the possibility of an, an event here similar to the December two thousand and twenty-five massacre in, in Bondi, in Sydney.

So relating this to the Arms Bill, I want to draw your attention to changes in wording which appear to increase the risks for the Jewish community, not reduce them. In Clause 3, Section B of the bill, right at the beginning, the wording is, "Impose controls to prevent the possession and use of arms for criminal activity."

This is actually looser than the wording of the present act. The present Clause 1A of the act particularly balances the individual privilege of possession and use of arms against the individual responsibility of acting in the interests of personal and public safety, and this is no longer expressed so precisely in the bill.

For instance, hate speech is not a crime in New Zealand, but our history shows that hate speech against Jews morphs into hate action against Jews. Linking arms possession and use to criminality, and more specifically to gangs, bypasses the emphasis on responsibility for public safety that exists in the present act.

It's that emphasis that provide better protection for the Jewish community and for other minority ethnic and religious communities. I support the submission from Gun Control New Zealand that calls for a rewording of Clause 3 in the bill.Along the lines of saying, quote, "Impose controls on the possession and use of arms for personal and public safety," close quote.

There are other detailed recommendations in the Gun Control New Zealand submission that have a bearing on the protection of the Jewish community within the umbrella of public safety that I support. Nami hinui shalom. Thank you very much. Um, can I just... While people are digesting that, you say specific- I'm opposed to allowing vetting period for pest controls from two and a half to five years.

Why, why is that specifically about pest controllers? Obviously, pest- I'm sorry, I, I didn't hear you. Oh, sorry. In your submission, you say, "I'm opposed to, to the following changes," and you list three of them, but you're saying extending the vetting periods for pest controllers from two and a half to five years. Why do you specifically target the pest controllers? Do you think they're a high risk?

No. It will be a very small group. I, I... Um, so I think it's just a question of, of ex- Sorry, I'm re- That's all right. That's all right. Should've done that before. Uh, no, I don't think pest controllers individually are, are a greater risk. I just think that- That's not- As in other parts of the changes in the bill, it, it, it, it shows an, an extension of what exists now.

Yeah. Okay. Not, not, n- certainly not directly personally against pest controllers. And your, o- the other last submission, change, you're s- you're say- suggesting mandatory reporting, requiring license holders to report other license holders whom they believe pose a risk to themselves or others. You're saying that's a mandatory widespread, requirement that should be imposed on all rifle owners or fire, license holders?

I, I used to work in the, in the educational field. Mm. And I remember the controversy about teachers making mandatory reports when they saw children who had been coming to class with bruises or some sort- Yeah... as, as an indication of, of physical abuse in the home.

And I think that that aspect of being aware that another person, your friend, may be having some changes in his attitudes that will lead to something dangerous is something that I think is, is well worth incorporating. Okay. All right. I, I think we have the same in, within the bill or within the...

I'm not sure within the Present Act on requiring doctors to report, wounds from, from the use of arms. Right. Uh, it's an indication. It's not, it's not a direct accusation, but it's just to, to indicate a, a feeling that something might be going wrong. People change, and if some- sometimes they start showing, degrees of, of difference of opinion, that might become violent.

And, and if this is... If there is some awareness of that, then the, the, the, the Jewish community will be safer as a result. All right. Hey, well, thank you for your submission. Right. Um, we're going to, Mr. Selwyn McCallum. Yes. Um, in person, hopefully.

Yeah. You're right there. Right. Nice to see you, Mr. McCallum. Thank you. You've got five minutes. We've seen your submission. Yep. Yep. Look, my primary reason for being here today is regarding airguns. Um, they're my area of expertise and my area of interest. Currently, the act, the proposed act defines the airgun as anything by which, by the use of gas or compressed air discharges any bull- bullet, missile, or other projectile.

That basically covers everything from a pop gun to a Nerf gun to a spud gun. Anything, literally, you squeeze a bulb and it fires a... If I... A straw, a plastic straw with a spitball. The use of the compressed air in my lungs becomes a weapon under this act. It's ridiculous. It says that, other than exempted f- other than exempted airguns can be made by regulation.

Why are we adding another layer of regulation, another layer of, bureaucracy to a system that can be easily solved simply by going the UK model? We say anything under 2 joules, right? And 2 joules is the limit by which a projectile can do any meaningful harm. That's a toy. We know it's a toy.

Right? That covers all of Nerf guns, it covers gel blasters, it covers all of the current toy guns that you can find at any two-dollar shop in New Zealand. Anything between 2 joules and 18 joules is an air rifle. Anything ordinarily capable of over 18 joules is a firearm and is regulated as a firearm.

Mm. Now, that covers... And I choose 18 joules very deliberately because that's the upper end of what a paintball gun is capable of, and it's certainly above the limit that all commercial paintball gun fields set for their, for operation. That 18-joule limit catches everything from the current airgun I can buy at Gun City that uses a CO2 cylinder to fire a full-sized arrow faster than my compound hunting bow can fire one, and yet that's a no license required airgun under the current regulations and under what you're proposing.

If you set an upper limit of 18 joules or 16 joules, whatever the government decides is the appropriate point, and I would suggest 18 very strongly, at that point, it becomes a licensable firearmEvery arms dealer, every paintball field, every airsoft field, every firearms range, and I would say a good sixty percent of firearms owners own a chronograph. No bigger than a mobile phone and a trivial cost.

If the police take a weapon into, into custody, they run a five-shot string over it with a, with a commercially available chronograph, that gets recorded as evidence at the time of seizure, and then there's no questions about what it was when the police seized it. It takes away the need for restrictive and confusing orders in council. It takes away the need for regulations to... And I don't think the current government or any government could actually list all of the various gun toys that are out there that are still sold in every two dollar shop, every Kmart,

adequately to prevent them being defined as an air rifle under the current act. But with simple changes, I believe you could. Right. Now, I've got a, a question from my good colleague here, Mr. O'Connor. Yeah, thanks. Right. Can you see an issue with air-- those air pistols and the air rifles, in that you talked about being able to go and test them later on at the station, but how do you know what the strength or what the, that pistol at the time? You- usually gonna be seizing them on the street at a search situation. We're gonna be seizing them at a search warrant.

Um, so if you don't... You wouldn't be able to seize them to go and test them, would you? If you- Yes, you would. If they're being seized- On what, on what grounds could you seize them? The, a belief that they were being used in criminal activity. If you've got a warrant or you've pulled over someone at a stop, and they've got an air rifle in the car, right? The current act already says they're deemed to be in possession of it.

If an officer has reasonable belief that that could be a firearm or, or a more powerful weapon, they've got every right to seize it and, and check it. If they're wrong, it gets returned. If they believe it was used in criminal activity, then regardless of whether it's a firearm, look, it looks like a firearm, under the legislation, it's treated as a firearm for the purposes of prosecution.

But remember, the possession in itself is the criminal offense and, when it comes to a possession of an illegal firearm- And that, that is where the police, that's where the police have to use their judgment, or the legislation has to have room for, much like we do with cars, illegally modified cars. We can seize them, we can get them tested.

If we're wrong, we return them. Right? We're talking about things that pose danger to human life. Being able to turn around and go, "I'm sorry, after, after examination, this was not," and returning it to the person if they're, if they're appro- if it's appropriate- Yeah... is better than allowing those weapons into, the wrong hands.

Police make mistakes. Everyone makes mistakes. It's better to have a law that sets a clear definable limit and if the police have got reason for concern, they seize it, they test it. If it's a firearm, it becomes a possession offense. If it's not a firearm, they've already raided the place, they've probably got more than enough offenses on the book already.

Yeah. Okay. Which actually, if I may, just one quick other one. Your definition of restricted firearm or restricted airgun, if I may be blunt, is patently ludicrous. Every airgun to a layman looks like a firearm. There is no such thing as an airgun that if presented in a dark alley or presented at ten meters does not sufficiently resemble a firearm to concern a police officer.

Yeah. I, I carried a gun for a living for twenty years in Australia. Believe me, if it looks like a gun, it's a gun until you, until you're made, made it safe. Yeah. So having a separate rule for restricted airgun, and then saying, "Resembles a firearm," is an unnecessary piece of legislation because every airgun- Okay. All right. Hey, thank you. We, we had the paintball people at weekend, week before. They were talking about up to seventeen joules, so you're in line with that. Thank you for your submission.

No, thank you. Right. Now, Simon, Nelson. Mr. Simon Nelson, you have been waiting patiently for a while. That's all right. You know the drill. Yeah. Been here before, unfortunately. Well, not unfortunately. You don't have to say that. Well, let's hope we get a result this time. All right. I... My name's Simon Nelson. Um, thank you for the opportunity today.

Um, I've been involved with firearms for over forty years, both professionally and personally. Um, I take part in many disciplines, including hunting, target shooting, collecting, and I am the current president of the Hawke's Bay branch of the Antique Arms Association. Um, I would like to say that on a whole, I support the rewrite of the act, but there is a few things, points I'd like to raise, obviously.

Um, I do fully support the removal of the police from administering the act, from having any control of the regulator. Um, it's time for this to change and we put in place a system that all New Zealanders, licensed and non-licensed, can believe in. Um, one of the things I'd like to te- it's probably a contentious issue, is around prohibited firearms. Um, at the moment, I believe it's, it could be tidied up. You have many categories of P endorsement which all end up in the same place.

If we wrap them all up in one, 'cause the vetting, the storage, the only thing that changes at the end is the use. So you, people still have access to, you know, they have access to them, which I believe is probably your primary concern as far as safety goes. And, and this is not talking about the proliferation of them or more of them being out in the community, but the fact is that they are out there and held within-- Certain people do have the right to hold these items, and it's about getting... I would like to see something akin to the old

ECAPSo it's not a watering down of the vetting process, it's not a watering down of the security process. It's none of that. It merely means that if we are applying to r- hold these items, that you go through that process, and at the end of it you're deemed a fit and proper person to both have possession of them, and I can see no real harm in then if you have possession of them, to have use of them.

So, so how does that licensing work at the moment? When you apply for a P category endorsement, you apply under several categories, um- What are the categories? Um, ooh, heirloom, collector, museum- Yes... theater, and yep, pest control, uh... Hang on, what's the other one?

It's all the pen endorsements, magazines. Yeah. Oh, yeah, and a separate... Yeah, 'cause I count those as separate, and there's a pen endorsement for them. So they're all, they're all different categories. They're all different categories, and you apply- And you're saying they should all be put in under a P category. Yes, but you state- Which would mean you've gotta be probably, probably more inten- intensely vetted. But the vetting is the same for all of them now.

Yeah. Okay. You know? Because you're gonna have access to these things. Right. And that's, that's... Which is fair enough, okay? Because for whatever reason you want to hold this item, the fact is you will end up having access to it if granted a, an endorsement. So why then do at the end we only say that only one particular group can use it when the risk factor is the access to them, not the use?

If you're following my- Mm-hmm. Okay... my theory. Yeah, we do. So in that way, 'cause if you look at what... You know, you can have a P1, a P2, and P3, and a P... You know? But they don't ultimately change anything. Your reason for having it, and you can still put that down in your application, but you in... The end result would be you'd all get to the same place if granted that.

Um, does that answer that one? Yeah, that's good. Oh, yeah. Um, the... Secondly, I'd like to bring up about, in the act I'd like to see an amnesty for- Yeah... unregistered, and I brought that up 'cause that's a big one. Mm-hmm. I mean, I'm going through that at the moment, and it's a pain. If, if we are serious about registering firearms, then we need to put in place a process that enables people- Mm-hmm... to willingly come forward. Um, I've taken possession... I, I know this is going on.

I've taken possession of two handguns that have been unregistered for... One of them for over 100 years. Comes from the family, brought it back. It's been in the family's possession from the First World War, never been registered. The drama I've got with trying to get that registered and the attitude of the FSA people, and process at the moment, 'cause I have to find both a dealer and also that dealer has to have a personal endorsement on their license under which to hold that item, and then they then have to go to the

FSA and the police and apply to get it registered. Right. Okay. And I think it'd be a lot simpler if we said that if you hold an endorsement for the particular item, whether it's a rifle or a handgun or whatever, and you can bring it forward, there'd be no... Granted, I accept that if it turns out that it's been used for nefarious or illegal means, that you would have to fess up where it came from and-... I'm hopeful you will. I've said that to these people.

If this turns out that it's been stolen or you've told me a story, I've believed it, I've taken it to the FSA. Okay. But if it doesn't, I'm coming straight back to you. Yeah. You know? All right. I think we've heard that one. Yeah. Yeah. Cool. Um, and if you can, I know, I know it's not politically acceptable at the moment. I, I don't believe in the register. I've been involved with it now.

Okay. I think- Okay, cool. Yeah. Oh, yeah, I just think though that when you do it, can... Is there any way that this committee can get the register changed to be more user-friendly? Oh, we've been asking those questions, so. Yeah. 'Cause if you are gonna keep it, it needs to be better. All right. Cool. In order to encourage people. I'm just conscious about time. Oh, sorry. But thank you. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much for your time. All right. Cheers. Excellent. Um, now we've got Mr. Conrad Peterson.

Good. Thank you for the opportunity to speak to my submission. Is it all right if I stand? Of course. Thank you. My concern is pure and simple. The controls, the current controls for cartridges, pellets for air guns, any kind of what I call ammunition, are way, way too loose in my view. What we need to see, in my opinion, and I'm basically, I'm just elaborating on what I've said in my submission, I personally think that we need the importation, the New Zealand

manufacture, or remanufacture of all types of ammunition for firearms or high-powered air guns to be far more controlled, and this includes the storage, any remanufacture, any sale. All right? My focus is on the ammunition, what I've... that very broad term.

You know, I don't care how many 3D guns you print. You cannot print 3D ammunition other than those slivers to put an ear off or something. The focus, in my view, needs to be not on the firearms themselves or their high-powered air guns. That's not the way to get better control.

So the other thing I would like to say, of course, is that in my view, this needs to go into regulation, not into legislation. There needs to be some real effort put into, getting it right.Now my last few years with the New Zealand Customs Service, I worked very closely with Parliamentary Counsel Office on legislation and regulations.

So I don't have any problem putting my money where my mouth is. And what I mean by that is the Parliamentary Counsel Office was to form a working group to go into doing, putting some proper regulation in place. I would happily put my hand up and work with that on a free of charge basis.

Right. We have to be very, very careful here that we don't get traditional people, like the black powder people, the bang and clouds of smoke, they should not be disadvantaged. They should not be disadvantaged. So there's quite a lot of care and effort goes into this.

I don't think for a moment that the majority of the firearms people will like me at all for what I'm proposing, 'cause in fact it's going to make life a lot more difficult for them. Right. Can I ask you a question? Please, by all means. Uh, Mr. Carl Bates.

Um, thanks very much Chair, and thanks for your submission. Um, by doing what you propose and as you say, making it more difficult, more costly, for legitimate firearm- Costly? You used the word costly... well, by adding regulation you're gonna add cost to the process. Ah.

You're not gonna do what you were- Cost-... you're proposing without increasing costs... excuse me, costly to who? Well, I'm asking the questions, and my question to you is, do you believe that doing this would actually stop, the person who's trying to clearly do something, that's against the law from actually, doing that?

You're saying will it stamp out crime effectively? My answer to that is of course no. We have I don't know how many thousands of police, as I seem to recall, and Mr. Otara will know. Well, there are some distinguished gentlemen, so... It's not, that's not gonna happen. So it's- Of course it's not... so, so what effectively would it solve if we, if we did what you propose?

It will effectively solve the immediate problem of people being able to manufacture... If I have a firearms license, I'm allowed to manufacture ammunition at home. No. And you think that, that, that people who are, using that ammunition for, challenging purposes would abide by this change?

Define challenging. Well, some of the challenging circumstances we've had, deadly circumstances we've had in New Zealand in the last couple of decades, are those individuals gonna- I would answer that-... just not use ammunition or are they gonna find another way?

I would answer that question by saying, there will always be challenges to rules and regulations. What I'm endeavoring to do with my submission, and what I'm r- requesting that this committee consider, is just tightening the noose a little- Right... on round ammunition.

All right. Hey, thank you, Mr. Peterson. Um, as you say, it's a slightly different perspective, but an interesting perspective. Thank you for the opportunity. Thank you. Right, now we've got Mr. John Knight who has been waiting patiently from about 10 o'clock this morning.

Right. Five minutes with you, Mr. Knight. Tena koutou. Um, in my view, three words need adding to the purpose of this act, the words being under lawful circumstances or, or equivalent. Um, unless this is done, police are unable to use the Arms Act, the current one, to, prosecute people who misuse firearms in rural New Zealand for the common practice of poaching.

Now, I'm one of very few landowners, I've been a landowner for 45 years, I've been a firearm user for 60 years, I'm one of very few landowners who has managed to get police, in a position where they can successfully prosecute a poacher. That doesn't happen very often.

Um, I've spent, I spent more than three decades trying to stop poaching on our 230 hectare forest, and I've achieved this finally by installing 28 trail cameras and numerous posters provided by police. And I want to show you an enlarged version of the police poster.

Now, the interesting thing about this poster is the Arms Act is not mentioned. It, it's not mentioned. And the reason is that police cannot use Section 45 of the Arms Act because of a curious, in my view, ruling, on an appeal by Justice Wild, who stated, "Hunting wild deer is a lawful, proper, and sufficient purpose for having a rifle."Full stop.

Irrespective of whether that person was trespassing or in breach of the Wild Animal Control Act, the circumstances didn't matter. He was just taking a narrow reading of the purpose of this bill and saying, "That, you know, means that hunting wild deer is sufficient."

Um, the case, Daly versus Police, involved Mr. Daly and a, and a, co-conspirator were hunting on Ngamatea Station, for a week, poaching. Um, and, this appeal was allowed. So since then, the police have declined to use Section 45 of the Arms Act, in cases involving poaching.

Now, the stated purpose of the current bill is very similar to the stated purpose of the existing act. So the decision of Justice Wild is gonna carry over. It is going to neutralize the effectiveness of the new act unless something is done to add to the purpose.

Cool. Right. Yep. Professor, we got a question already. Uh, do you wanna... Have you got more you wanna say or no? Uh, well, I was gonna talk about Section 253 because- Oh, let's... it's very similar to-... Section 45- Yeah... of the existing act. Um, but it's gonna f- fall by the wayside for the same reason.

Uh, but the other thing about Section 253 is it specifically mentions a public place. Um- Yeah. Yeah. Now, what about private pla- what about private land? So it doesn't deal with poaching. That's right. Um, but since writing my submission, I've become aware of Section 256, which could cover poaching, except, commit an offense punishable by imprisonment for a term of three years. The Wild Animal Control Act is only two years. So unless that three years change to two,

this would not cover poaching. Okay. Questions? Um, the, my- You go for it. Um, who owns the wild deer on your land? The Crown. So really it shouldn't be about deer, it should be about trespass, shouldn't it? That's right. So the fact that they're poaching, so wouldn't it be a mistake to try and add poaching- Well-... to the Arms Act?

Well- Um, you're trying... Whereas this is a completely different purposes. No, but it's misuse of firearms, that's the issue. Well- The danger, the danger to me as a landowner, and to people who I allow on my land, the, the, the danger is the misuse of firearms on our land.

It, it's nothing to do with deer really, it's, you know, if you want to take that narrow view- Well, but my- It, it's the fact that armed people are coming on our land. Po- poaching's a little bit to do with the deer, isn't it? Oh, yeah. So I'll just, I won't Well, that's the intention. That's why they- You can see that there are s- a few issues there... Yeah, but that's why they're there, but that's not...

The issue is man with gun in the bushes, possibly thinking you're a deer. You know, I, I find this extremely intimidating. Yeah. Well, I share your comments because I used to have poachers on my farm. And once I caught, caught one with the two of us, and he said, "If you do anything, I'm gonna come and shoot all your dogs."

Yeah. Rather than, um... And when you're standing in the middle of the bush- Yeah... wondering what's around the corner- Yeah... I know you'd fear it. Thank you. All right. Cool. Thank you, Professor. Um, very helpful. Uh, no one else has raised poaching. That's what we like, new stuff. Mm-hmm. Right. Thank you very much. Now we're gonna go on to David, Ala- Alard, who is by Zoom.

He's not. Mr. Quentin Abraham, right here waiting. Right. God, we've got a run of passion of people fronting up. It's great. Um- I've heard how good the chairman was. I... Tena koutou te komiti o takitini. Uh, I'm Quentin Abraham. I'm a psychologist. Um, I work with people with mental health difficulties. It's not uncommon for me to have people have suicidal thoughts or are suicidal.

Uh, and when guns are implicated, that's usually fatal. So, I'm not in favor of this bill, m- mainly because I would like to see the regulations tightened. I'd like the, the time period, to be, sort of shorter. I'd like to see the police directly involved with that.

I'd like the legislation to be carefully drafted and well considered. Um, the, it's estimated, like, over the course of a lifetime, one in two of us will have a mental health difficulty. Uh, a proportion of us, will be suicidal. Um, y- just- What sort of proportion?

So, in terms of the, the, the numbers per, per year, we're talking about, in terms of death, we're talking about 600 a year roughly. So we're talking relatively small numbers, but the impact on those communities-... often deprived, often, rural areas, often where there are access to guns.

And, we've got a lot of guns in this country, 1.7 million that we know that are registered. Uh, we've got 6.5 times more than Australia, 2.5 times more than the UK. Um, so I'm interested in there being a few of those circulated. Um, we notice when regulation was reduced-Uh, we had sort of, a, a reduction in the numbers of, of deaths by guns.

Um, I kind of... I think there was mention before about the relation- the, the similar regulation with cars. I have a partner, she's a doctor. She has to tell elderly people, "You're no longer able to drive a car." Um, she has to weigh up the risk for the individual rights of that person versus killing somebody on the road, and I, my own view is that's a very complex decision.

To make those kind of predictions, is that person at risk or not- Yeah... is a difficult choice. I'd like to see police officers or very well-trained people doing that, not somebody loosely trained who, who's not able to make that decision or have the manner to actually- Who d- who does... Who are you referring to when you say- So I'm referring to at the moment if we shift the, the decision about who can o- own, own a firearm to somebody who's not well qualified and who cannot make that decision, I don't think that's a g- uh-

So within the r- li- arms authority. Yes. Yes. Uh, you don't think they're qualified. So I don't think having a, a, an, an, an independent service that isn't, that does not have the manner of the police behind it is a good idea. Okay. And I don't also think that having a long period of time t- for that decision to be made is a good idea because I deal with people, you know, they're middle-aged, they've got a drinking problem, their marriage is going down the toilet, they've spent some money unwisely. They've got a gun under the bed.

Uh, it's not locked in a storage ca- They've not got f- the facility to make sure that's locked up properly. Uh, I'd like, the police, who I'm gonna have to ring to sort that out, to actually be responsible for ens- you know, making that decision prior- Okay... to that. Now we've got a few questions, so we're banking up.

Yes. Right. Mr. Todd Simpson first. So thank you. Yeah. I mean, we've obviously heard a lot about, mental health. Yeah. Um, but it seems to be what you're actually submitting would actually stop people seeking mental health. Would that- Uh-... support? Because y- so you're in favor- N-... of mandatory reporting- Yes... with a firearm.

Yes. Yes. And so you don't see this as un- And I'm, I'm in favor of actually preventing it well in advance- Right... by not having as many guns circulating. No, no, that's not my question. Uh, yeah. So you're f- you, you're in favor of mandatory reporting if someone comes to you with a mental health- I am... concern. Yes. Okay. So secondly then, how do you balance that with, I suppose, the healthcare professional responsibility around patient confidentiality and to actually help the- It's a really difficult-... person in front of you?

It's a really difficult problem. Uh, you said you had the h- And, and I, I have to make, I have to make that choice. So if that, if that occurs, I do have to actually report that. Um- So do you, do you operate under the Hippocratic Oath as a psychologist? We, we have a, a code of ethics wh- that requires us if somebody is a danger to themselves or someone else, we have to report.

Right. So what I don't want is to have to be making that decision all the time, 'cause it's a very difficult decision to make. So if we have less guns around, less cost of those guns being registered- That's one solution... I then don't have to make that s- that decision. Isn't... Don't you think that actually a trained healthcare professional is- We trust it... the right person to decide? 'Cause we heard evidence this morning-... from a doctor that they're doing this all the time.

They can actually decide if the person-... in front of them is of- Absolutely... is a danger to themselves- Yes... or others, and they're- So I'm saying I, I think it's better that police officers are making decision. If there, if there is a gun and they've got to actually legislate for... If, if they've actually got to deal with all that guns to keep those off the street, I don't think they're going to listen to me when I say, "Oh, I don't, I don't think that's a very good idea."
